name: Tipton Station
description: Tipton, Missouri served as the eastern terminus of the Butterfield Overland Mail route beginning in 1858, where passengers arriving by the Pacific Railroad transferred to stagecoaches for the grueling 2,800-mile journey to San Francisco. The small depot town buzzed with activity as mail, passengers, and freight were loaded onto Concord coaches and celerity wagons bound for the western frontier. Stagecoach stations like Tipton saw heavy foot traffic and commerce for years, making them prime locations for finding period coins, buttons, and personal effects lost by thousands of travelers passing through over the years.

name: Springfield, MO
description: Springfield, Missouri was a major station on the Butterfield Overland Mail route where coaches stopped for fresh teams and passengers took meals during the multi-day journey westward. The town served as an important provisioning point where stage operators maintained corrals, stock, and repair facilities for the demanding overland service. Metal detectorists find this a rewarding site as the constant flow of travelers, teamsters, and station workers over several years resulted in numerous lost coins, buttons, pocket items, and horse tack scattered around the old station grounds.

name: Fayetteville, AR
description: Fayetteville, Arkansas served as a crucial stop on the Butterfield Overland Mail route as coaches wound through the Ozark Mountains on their way to Indian Territory and Texas. The station provided a welcome rest for passengers after navigating the challenging mountain terrain, with facilities for changing teams and taking refreshment. The heavy traffic through this station between 1858 and 1861 makes it an excellent metal detecting location, with coins, personal items, and stage hardware frequently lost in the station yard and surrounding areas.

name: Fort Smith, AR
description: Fort Smith, Arkansas was one of the most important stops on the Butterfield Overland Mail route, serving as the last major settlement before entering Indian Territory to the west. The military fort and adjacent civilian town provided substantial facilities for the stage line including corrals, shops, and lodging, with coaches arriving and departing on strict schedules. As both a military installation and busy stagecoach junction operating for years, Fort Smith offers exceptional metal detecting potential with military artifacts, stage-related hardware, and countless personal items from soldiers, travelers, and settlers concentrated in the historic fort and station areas.

name: Colbert's Ferry
description: Colbert's Ferry was the critical Red River crossing point for the Butterfield Overland Mail where coaches transferred from Arkansas into Texas via ferry barge operated by the Chickasaw Nation. This bottleneck location saw every westbound and eastbound coach wait for river crossing, with passengers disembarking while their vehicles were ferried across the often-turbulent waters. The combination of a busy ferry operation and stage station makes this site highly productive for metal detecting, with lost coins, buttons, and personal effects from thousands of anxious travelers concentrated along the riverbanks and waiting areas.

name: Sherman, TX
description: Sherman, Texas served as an important relay station on the Butterfield Overland Mail route as it crossed North Texas, providing fresh horses and brief respite for passengers on the demanding overland journey. The station maintained corrals and stock facilities to support the regular schedule of eastbound and westbound coaches passing through the growing Texas settlement. Metal detectorists value these Texas prairie stations where dropped coins, harness fittings, and traveler's personal items accumulated around the busy relay facilities during the line's years of operation.

name: Gainesville, TX
description: Gainesville, Texas operated as a Butterfield Overland Mail relay station in North Texas, where stage crews changed horses and passengers stretched their legs during brief stops. The station sat along the critical route segment connecting Fort Smith to the Texas frontier forts farther west, seeing regular traffic from 1858 to 1861. Relay stations like Gainesville are prime metal detecting sites where the constant activity of hostlers, drivers, and passengers over several years resulted in numerous lost coins, buttons, and stage hardware around the corrals and station buildings.

name: Fort Belknap, TX
description: Fort Belknap, Texas served as a vital Butterfield Overland Mail station at the abandoned military fort on the Texas frontier, providing protection and facilities in an area threatened by Comanche raids. The stage company utilized the existing fort buildings for their operations, maintaining horses, equipment, and armed guards to protect coaches traveling through this dangerous section of the route. The combination of military and stage operations makes Fort Belknap exceptional for metal detecting, with army buttons, buckles, weapons parts, horseshoes, and traveler's coins scattered across the fort grounds and station yard.

name: Fort Chadbourne, TX
description: Fort Chadbourne, Texas was a key Butterfield Overland Mail station located at another abandoned military post in West Texas, where coaches stopped for fresh teams and passengers found shelter at this isolated frontier outpost. The stage line converted the fort's remaining structures into station facilities, maintaining stock and equipment needed to traverse the arid country between settlements. Metal detectorists prize these combined fort-and-station sites where military artifacts like buttons, cartridges, and buckles mix with stage-era coins, hardware, and personal items from years of overland traffic.

name: Fort Concho, TX
description: Fort Concho near San Angelo, Texas served as an important Butterfield Overland Mail station where the route passed through this key military installation protecting West Texas settlements and trails. The active fort provided security for stage operations, with coaches stopping to change teams and passengers receiving protection from hostile encounters in this remote region. The extensive military and civilian activity at Fort Concho creates outstanding metal detecting opportunities, with army artifacts, stage hardware, coins, and personal effects concentrated around the fort buildings and adjacent station grounds.

name: Fort Stockton, TX
description: Fort Stockton, Texas operated as a crucial stop for both the Butterfield Overland Mail and the San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line, making it one of the most important stage stations in West Texas. The military fort and stage facilities provided security, fresh horses, and supplies for coaches traversing the dangerous desert country between San Antonio and El Paso. The dual-route traffic and military presence make Fort Stockton an exceptional metal detecting site, with abundant military buttons, weapons parts, horseshoes, harness fittings, and coins from soldiers, teamsters, and passengers lost over many years of operations.

name: El Paso, TX
description: El Paso, Texas served as a major junction where the Butterfield Overland Mail and San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line both stopped, making it one of the most important stage stations in the Southwest. The town's strategic location at the Rio Grande crossing provided extensive facilities for multiple stage lines, with corrals, shops, hotels, and supply depots supporting regular coach traffic from three directions. As a major transportation hub operating for years with heavy traffic from both routes, El Paso offers remarkable metal detecting potential with period coins, buttons, stage hardware, and personal items concentrated in the historic downtown and station areas.

name: Tucson, AZ
description: Tucson, Arizona was a major station on both the Butterfield Overland Mail and San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line, serving as the most important settlement in southern Arizona Territory. The old Spanish presidio town provided substantial facilities for stage operations including corrals, repair shops, and lodging, with coaches arriving from California, Texas, and Mexico. The convergence of multiple stage routes and years of continuous operation make Tucson exceptional for metal detecting, with Spanish, Mexican, and American coins, military artifacts, and stage-related items scattered throughout the historic presidio district and station grounds.

name: Yuma, AZ
description: Yuma, Arizona was the critical Colorado River crossing point for the Butterfield Overland Mail, where coaches transferred across the river via ferry after the long desert journey from Tucson. This bottleneck location saw every coach stop for the river crossing, with passengers waiting while their vehicles and teams were ferried across the powerful current to Fort Yuma on the California side. The concentration of travelers at this essential crossing makes it highly productive for metal detecting, with coins, buttons, and personal effects lost along the riverbanks and ferry landing areas during years of stage operations.

name: Fort Yuma, CA
description: Fort Yuma, California sat on the California side of the Colorado River crossing where Butterfield Overland Mail coaches arrived after ferrying from Yuma, with the military post providing security and facilities for westbound travelers entering California. The fort's strategic location protecting the vital river crossing made it a hub of activity with soldiers, stage passengers, and freight wagons all passing through this gateway to California. The combination of military installation and major stage station creates excellent metal detecting opportunities, with army buttons, coins, stage hardware, and traveler's effects concentrated around the fort grounds and landing areas.

name: San Bernardino, CA
description: San Bernardino, California served as an important Butterfield Overland Mail station where coaches stopped after crossing the Mojave Desert, with the Mormon settlement providing welcome facilities and fresh teams for the final leg to Los Angeles. The station maintained extensive corrals and equipment to support the regular schedule of coaches approaching the end of their transcontinental journey from Missouri. Metal detectorists value this site where the relief of reaching California civilization after desert crossings led to celebration and commerce, with period coins, buttons, and personal items lost around the busy station and town center.

name: Los Angeles, CA
description: Los Angeles, California served as the western terminus of the Butterfield Overland Mail from 1858 to 1860, where coaches completed their 25-day, 2,800-mile journey from Missouri and passengers celebrated reaching the Pacific coast. The growing pueblo town's station facilities handled arriving and departing coaches, mail sorting, and the transfer of passengers continuing to San Francisco by coastal steamer. As a major stage terminus with years of heavy traffic, Los Angeles offers remarkable metal detecting potential with period coins, buttons, personal effects, and stage-related items concentrated in the historic pueblo district and station grounds.

name: San Antonio, TX
description: San Antonio, Texas was the eastern terminus of the San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line (the 'Jackass Mail') beginning in 1857, where mail and passengers departed on the grueling journey to California via the southern desert route. The substantial facilities in this frontier military hub supported the semi-monthly mail service with pack mules and light coaches, serving as the jumping-off point for travelers heading west through hostile territory. The heavy stage traffic combined with San Antonio's role as a major military and commercial center makes it exceptional for metal detecting, with Spanish, Mexican, and American coins, military artifacts, and traveler's items throughout the historic downtown area.

name: Fort Clark, TX
description: Fort Clark near Brackettville, Texas was an essential San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line station where coaches stopped at this active military post for security and fresh teams along the dangerous Texas frontier. The fort provided armed escorts and protection for mail coaches traveling through territory threatened by Apache and Comanche raids between San Antonio and the Rio Grande. The combination of military garrison and stage operations creates excellent metal detecting opportunities, with army buttons, weapons parts, horseshoes, coins, and stage hardware concentrated around the fort grounds and station facilities.

name: Fort Davis, TX
description: Fort Davis, Texas served as a critical San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line station in the rugged Davis Mountains of West Texas, where the military post provided security and facilities for coaches traveling through Apache territory. The fort's strategic location in the mountains made it an essential stop for changing teams and obtaining armed escorts before continuing through the dangerous desert country to El Paso. Metal detectorists prize Fort Davis for its rich combination of military and stage artifacts, with army buttons, coins, weapons parts, and stage hardware scattered across the extensive fort grounds and station areas.

name: San Diego, CA
description: San Diego, California was the western terminus of the San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line from 1857 to 1861, where mail and passengers completed their journey across the southwestern deserts from Texas. The growing coastal town's station facilities marked the end of the first regular mail service to California, with coaches and pack trains arriving after weeks of travel through hostile territory. As a stage line terminus with years of operations, San Diego offers good metal detecting potential around the historic Old Town area where station facilities, hotels, and mail offices concentrated the traffic of arriving and departing travelers.

name: Atchison, KS
description: Atchison, Kansas served as the eastern terminus of the Central Overland California & Pike's Peak Express and its successor the Holladay Overland Mail from 1860 to 1866, where passengers boarded coaches for the 1,900-mile journey to Sacramento via the Platte River route. The bustling Missouri River town maintained extensive stage facilities including corrals, shops, offices, and hotels serving as the jumping-off point for travelers heading to California and the Pony Express operations. As a major overland terminus with years of heavy traffic from multiple stage lines, Atchison offers exceptional metal detecting potential with period coins, buttons, stage hardware, and personal effects throughout the historic riverfront and downtown districts.

name: Fort Kearny, NE
description: Fort Kearny, Nebraska was one of the most important stops on the Central Overland California & Pike's Peak Express and Holladay Overland Mail routes, serving as a major military post protecting emigrant trails and stage lines along the Platte River. The fort provided security, supplies, and fresh horses for coaches heading west to California, with extensive facilities supporting both military and stage operations. The years of combined military garrison and heavy stage traffic make Fort Kearny an outstanding metal detecting site, with army buttons, coins, weapons parts, horseshoes, harness fittings, and traveler's personal items scattered across the fort grounds and station areas.

name: Julesburg, CO
description: Julesburg, Colorado was a vital relay station on the Central Overland and Holladay routes where coaches stopped for fresh teams at this important junction along the South Platte River. The station's strategic location made it a hub of activity with stage operations, trading posts, and telegraph facilities all concentrated at this key point on the overland trail. Relay stations like Julesburg are exceptionally productive for metal detecting, with the constant changing of teams and flow of passengers resulting in abundant horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, buttons, and personal effects lost around the corrals and station buildings during years of operations.

name: Fort Laramie, WY
description: Fort Laramie, Wyoming was a major stop on the Central Overland and Holladay Overland routes, serving as one of the most important military posts protecting emigrant trails and stage lines in the West. The substantial fort provided security, supplies, and repair facilities for coaches traveling between Fort Kearny and South Pass, with the garrison protecting stage operations from hostile encounters. The extensive military and stage activity over many years makes Fort Laramie one of the finest metal detecting sites on the overland routes, with abundant army buttons, coins, weapons parts, stage hardware, and personal items from soldiers, passengers, and emigrants concentrated throughout the fort grounds.

name: South Pass, WY
description: South Pass, Wyoming marked the Continental Divide crossing for the Central Overland and Holladay routes, where coaches reached the summit of the Rocky Mountains at this relatively gentle passage used by emigrants and stage lines. The critical location required relay stations on both approaches to the pass, with facilities for changing exhausted teams after the climb and preparing for the descent into the Green River country. The concentration of stage traffic at this geographic landmark creates good metal detecting opportunities, with coins, buttons, and stage hardware lost around the relay stations and resting areas where thousands of travelers paused at the symbolic crossing.

name: Fort Bridger, WY
description: Fort Bridger, Wyoming was a major supply and relay stop on the Holladay Overland Mail route, where the fort and trading post founded by mountain man Jim Bridger provided crucial facilities in southwestern Wyoming. The military post and stage station supplied fresh horses, repairs, and provisions for coaches before the long haul to Salt Lake City through the Wasatch Mountains. The combination of trading post history, military occupation, and years of stage operations makes Fort Bridger exceptional for metal detecting, with trade goods, military artifacts, coins, stage hardware, and emigrant items scattered across the extensive fort and station grounds.

name: Salt Lake City, UT
description: Salt Lake City, Utah served as a major hub on the Central Overland and Holladay Overland Mail routes, where coaches stopped in the Mormon capital for substantial facilities including hotels, corrals, shops, and supplies midway on the transcontinental journey. The city's strategic location made it an essential provisioning point with stage operations, freight companies, and telegraph offices all maintaining significant presence to support overland traffic. The years of heavy stage and freight traffic through Salt Lake City create outstanding metal detecting opportunities, with period coins, buttons, stage hardware, and personal effects concentrated in the historic downtown district and station areas.

name: Carson City, NV
description: Carson City, Nevada was an important Central Overland California & Pike's Peak Express stop in the eastern Sierra Nevada, where coaches paused at this booming silver mining town before crossing the mountains to Sacramento. The town's facilities provided fresh horses, repairs, and lodging for passengers nearing the end of their transcontinental journey from Kansas. The combination of stage traffic and silver mining activity makes Carson City a productive metal detecting site, with period coins, mining artifacts, stage hardware, and personal effects from travelers and miners concentrated in the historic downtown and station areas.

name: Sacramento, CA
description: Sacramento, California served as the western terminus of the Central Overland California & Pike's Peak Express from 1860 to 1862, where coaches completed their journey across the continent and Pony Express riders arrived with mail from the East. The capital city's extensive stage facilities handled arriving and departing coaches, with hotels, corrals, and offices supporting the overland operations including the famous Pony Express. As a major transportation terminus with years of stage traffic and California's capital, Sacramento offers exceptional metal detecting potential with period coins, stage hardware, and personal items throughout the historic Old Sacramento district and riverfront areas.

name: Kansas City, MO
description: Kansas City, Missouri was the eastern terminus of the Overland Stage to Denver, where passengers departed on coaches bound for the Colorado gold fields via the Smoky Hill Trail or Kansas Pacific route. The bustling frontier town on the Missouri River maintained stage facilities to support the regular service to Denver, with prospectors, settlers, and businessmen all passing through on their way to Colorado Territory. The heavy traffic from multiple stage lines and years of operations make Kansas City a productive metal detecting site, with period coins, buttons, and personal effects concentrated in the historic downtown riverfront and station districts.

name: Junction City, KS
description: Junction City, Kansas served as an important stop on the Overland Stage to Denver, where coaches paused at this Kansas River town near Fort Riley for fresh teams and supplies. The town's location at the junction of multiple trails made it a hub for stage operations, military traffic, and emigrant parties heading to Colorado. Stage stops like Junction City offer good metal detecting potential, with coins, buttons, and personal items from travelers and station workers accumulated around the town center and station facilities during the Colorado gold rush years.

name: Fort Riley, KS
description: Fort Riley, Kansas was a major military post near the Overland Stage to Denver route, where the stage line benefited from the fort's protection and facilities as coaches traveled across Kansas toward Colorado Territory. The substantial cavalry post maintained extensive operations to protect trails and settlements, with stage coaches often stopping nearby for security and fresh horses. The military installation's years of operations create excellent metal detecting opportunities, with army buttons, coins, weapons parts, cavalry equipment, and stage-related artifacts scattered throughout the fort grounds and surrounding areas.

name: Fort Hays, KS
description: Fort Hays, Kansas was a critical stop on the Overland Stage to Denver route, where coaches stopped at this important military post protecting stage lines and railroads across western Kansas. The fort provided security, supplies, and fresh teams for coaches traveling through territory threatened by Indian raids during the 1860s conflicts on the plains. The combination of military garrison and stage operations makes Fort Hays an outstanding metal detecting site, with army buttons, coins, cavalry equipment, weapons parts, horseshoes, and stage hardware concentrated around the fort buildings and station areas.

name: Salina, KS
description: Salina, Kansas operated as a stop on the Overland Stage to Denver, where coaches paused in this growing Kansas town for fresh horses and supplies during the journey across the central plains. The town's facilities supported stage operations with corrals and equipment needed to maintain the regular schedule to Colorado Territory. Stage towns like Salina provide good metal detecting opportunities around the historic downtown and station areas, where coins, buttons, and personal items from travelers accumulated during the years of stage operations and frontier settlement.

name: Denver, CO
description: Denver, Colorado was the western terminus of the Overland Stage to Denver, where coaches arriving from Kansas City completed their journey to the booming gold rush capital of Colorado Territory. The city's stage facilities handled heavy traffic from multiple routes bringing prospectors, settlers, and supplies to the Rocky Mountain mining district throughout the 1860s and beyond. As a major stage terminus and mining boomtown, Denver offers exceptional metal detecting potential with period coins, mining artifacts, stage hardware, and personal effects concentrated in the historic downtown Larimer Street district and surrounding areas.

name: Plum Creek Station, NE
description: Plum Creek Station near present-day Lexington, Nebraska was an important Central Overland relay station along the Platte River route, where coaches stopped for fresh teams during the long haul across the Nebraska plains. The isolated relay station maintained corrals, stock, and minimal facilities to support the regular coach schedule, with station keepers living in constant vigilance for Indian raids during the 1860s conflicts. Relay stations like Plum Creek are prime metal detecting sites where horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and personal items from station workers and passengers accumulated around the corrals and buildings during years of stage operations.

name: Cottonwood Springs, NE
description: Cottonwood Springs in the North Platte area of Nebraska served as a Central Overland relay station where coaches stopped for fresh horses at this important location along the emigrant trail. The station's position near abundant water and grass made it a vital stopping point, with facilities to support both stage operations and the thousands of emigrants passing through on the Oregon and California trails. The concentration of trail traffic and stage operations creates excellent metal detecting opportunities, with coins, buttons, emigrant items, and stage hardware lost around the station grounds and camping areas during peak travel years.

name: Scotts Bluff, NE
description: Scotts Bluff, Nebraska was a Central Overland stage stop near this famous landmark on the emigrant trails, where coaches paused at relay stations positioned to serve the heavy traffic passing the distinctive bluffs. The dramatic geological formation served as a waypoint for both stage lines and emigrant trains, with facilities clustered near this recognizable feature on the plains. The combination of stage and emigrant trail traffic makes the Scotts Bluff area productive for metal detecting, with period coins, emigrant items, buttons, and stage hardware scattered around the historic trails and station sites.

name: Horseshoe Station, WY
description: Horseshoe Station, Wyoming was a Central Overland relay station positioned between Fort Laramie and South Pass, where coaches stopped for fresh teams in the remote country north of the Laramie Mountains. The isolated relay station required hardy station keepers to maintain horses and facilities in dangerous territory, with minimal shelter and constant threat from Indian raids during the 1860s. Remote relay stations like Horseshoe are excellent metal detecting sites where horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, buttons, and equipment from station operations accumulated around the corrals and buildings during the years of stage service.

name: Sweetwater Station, WY
description: Sweetwater Station on the Sweetwater River in Wyoming was an important Central Overland relay station where coaches stopped for fresh teams along this critical water source on the high plains approach to South Pass. The station's location along the river made it a vital stopping point with facilities to support stage operations in the remote country between Fort Laramie and the Continental Divide. Relay stations on the Sweetwater are productive for metal detecting, with horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and personal items from passengers and station workers scattered around the historic station site and river crossing areas.

name: Green River Crossing, WY
description: Green River Crossing, Wyoming was a critical Holladay Overland Mail station where coaches forded or ferried across the Green River on the route between South Pass and Fort Bridger. The challenging river crossing required substantial facilities including ferry operations during high water, with coaches and teams carefully transferred across the powerful current. River crossings are exceptional metal detecting sites where the concentration of waiting travelers and crossing operations led to numerous lost coins, buttons, and personal effects scattered along the riverbanks and ferry landing areas during years of stage traffic.

name: Echo Canyon Station, UT
description: Echo Canyon Station in Utah Territory was a Holladay Overland Mail relay station positioned in the dramatic Echo Canyon on the approach to Salt Lake City through the Wasatch Mountains. The station's location in the narrow canyon provided facilities for changing teams after the mountain crossing, with the dramatic red rock walls making it a memorable stop for travelers. Relay stations in mountain canyons are productive metal detecting sites, with horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and personal items from passengers and station operations concentrated in the limited flat areas around the buildings and corrals.

name: Egan Station, NV
description: Egan Station in Egan Canyon, Nevada was a remote Central Overland relay station where coaches stopped for fresh teams in the isolated desert ranges of eastern Nevada. The station maintained minimal facilities to support operations in this harsh environment, with station keepers enduring extreme isolation and the constant threat of Indian raids during the 1860s conflicts. Remote desert relay stations like Egan are excellent metal detecting sites where horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and equipment accumulated around the station grounds during the years of operations, with artifacts well-preserved in the dry climate.

name: Ruby Valley Station, NV
description: Ruby Valley Station in Nevada was an important Central Overland relay station positioned in the Ruby Valley where water and grass provided welcome relief after desert crossings. The station maintained facilities for changing teams and resting horses in this relatively hospitable location surrounded by arid mountains and alkali flats. Relay stations in valley locations are highly productive for metal detecting, with horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, buttons, and personal items from passengers and station workers scattered around the corrals, buildings, and water sources from years of stage traffic.

name: Dragoon Springs, AZ
description: Dragoon Springs in Arizona's Dragoon Mountains was a critical Butterfield Overland Mail station where coaches stopped for water at this rare spring in the hostile Apache territory of southeastern Arizona. The isolated station operated under constant threat of attack, with several station keepers killed in Apache raids during the route's operation from 1858 to 1861. The violent history and remote location make Dragoon Springs a fascinating metal detecting site, with potential for stage hardware, coins, buttons, and artifacts from both stage operations and the conflicts that occurred at this dangerous desert outpost.

name: Apache Pass Station, AZ
description: Apache Pass Station near Fort Bowie, Arizona was one of the most dangerous Butterfield Overland Mail stations, positioned at the critical mountain pass and spring controlled by Chiricahua Apache under Cochise. The station's location at the essential water source in Apache Pass made it a strategic but perilous stop, with the Army eventually establishing Fort Bowie to protect the pass after numerous attacks. The combination of stage operations, military occupation, and Apache conflict makes Apache Pass exceptional for metal detecting, with stage hardware, military artifacts, coins, and battle-related items concentrated around the historic station and fort sites.

name: Picacho Pass, AZ
description: Picacho Pass, Arizona was a Butterfield Overland Mail relay station at this distinctive desert pass between Tucson and the Gila River crossing. The station provided fresh horses for coaches crossing the Arizona desert, with the dramatic Picacho Peak serving as a landmark visible for miles across the desert floor. Desert relay stations like Picacho are productive for metal detecting in the dry climate that preserves artifacts, with horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and stage hardware scattered around the station site from the years of operations.

name: Mesilla, NM
description: Mesilla, New Mexico was an important stop for both the Butterfield Overland Mail and San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line, making it a key junction town in southern New Mexico Territory. The old Mexican plaza town provided substantial facilities for both stage routes, with hotels, corrals, and supplies supporting coaches traveling between El Paso and Tucson. The convergence of multiple stage routes and years of operations make Mesilla an outstanding metal detecting site, with Spanish, Mexican, and American coins, stage hardware, and personal items concentrated in the historic plaza district and station areas.

name: Phantom Hill, TX
description: Fort Phantom Hill, Texas was a Butterfield Overland Mail station located at this abandoned military post on the Texas frontier, where the stage company utilized the remaining fort buildings for relay operations. The isolated station maintained horses and equipment in this remote location between Fort Belknap and Fort Chadbourne, with the stone ruins providing shelter for stage operations. Abandoned fort stations are excellent for metal detecting, with army buttons, coins, weapons parts, and stage-era artifacts including horseshoes and harness fittings scattered across the extensive fort grounds and station areas.

name: Jacksboro, TX
description: Jacksboro near Fort Richardson, Texas was a Butterfield Overland Mail stop where coaches paused at this military post and frontier town in North Texas. The fort provided security for stage operations in territory threatened by Comanche and Kiowa raids, with the garrison protecting both the stage line and local settlements. The combination of military and stage operations creates good metal detecting potential, with army buttons, coins, stage hardware, and personal items from soldiers and travelers scattered around the historic fort grounds and town center.

name: Emigrant Spring, OR
description: Emigrant Spring, Oregon was a stage stop on the route through the Blue Mountains, where coaches and emigrant wagons stopped for water at this essential spring in the challenging mountain crossing. The spring's location made it a natural gathering point for trail traffic, with stage facilities developing to support regular coach service through the mountains to the Columbia River. Mountain spring stations offer good metal detecting opportunities, with coins, buttons, emigrant items, and stage hardware concentrated around the water source and camping areas from years of combined stage and emigrant trail traffic.

name: Castle Rock Station, CO
description: Castle Rock Station, Colorado was an Overland Stage to Denver relay station positioned at this distinctive rock formation south of Denver on the route from Kansas. The station provided fresh horses for coaches on the final leg to Denver, with the prominent Castle Rock landmark serving as a waypoint visible across the Colorado plains. Relay stations approaching major destinations like Denver are productive for metal detecting, with horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and personal items from anxious passengers nearing journey's end accumulated around the corrals and station buildings.

name: Bijou Creek Station, CO
description: Bijou Creek Station, Colorado was an Overland Stage to Denver relay station on Bijou Creek, providing water and fresh teams for coaches crossing the dry country of eastern Colorado. The isolated station maintained minimal facilities to support operations in this remote location between Kansas settlements and Denver, with station keepers enduring harsh conditions on the high plains. Remote prairie relay stations like Bijou Creek are excellent metal detecting sites where horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and equipment from station operations accumulated around the creek and buildings during the Colorado gold rush years.

name: Solomon's Fork Station, KS
description: Solomon's Fork Station on the Solomon River in Kansas was an Overland Stage to Denver relay station where coaches stopped for fresh teams along this important water source in central Kansas. The station's position along the river made it a vital stopping point with corrals and facilities to support stage operations on the route to Colorado Territory. River valley relay stations are highly productive for metal detecting, with horseshoes, harness fittings, coins, and personal items scattered around the station grounds and river crossing areas from years of stage traffic during the Denver rush.

name: Smoky Hill Spring, KS
description: Smoky Hill Spring, Kansas was a critical Overland Stage to Denver relay station on the Smoky Hill route, where this rare spring provided essential water in the dry stretch of western Kansas. The spring's importance made it a vital stopping point for coaches traveling the challenging Smoky Hill Trail, with facilities maintained despite the isolation and danger from Indian raids during the 1860s. Desert spring stations are exceptional metal detecting sites where the concentration of thirsty travelers and stage operations led to coins, buttons, horseshoes, and personal effects accumulated around the precious water source during years of frontier traffic.
